steps.title: Identify the sampling design and its origin
steps.content: The experiment began with a key design choice: 80 students were selected at random from a certain high school. In statistical inference, random sampling is the method that permits generalization. The critical question is: from what population were these 80 students drawn? The answer is stated in the problem: from that particular high school. Random sampling allows results to be generalized only to the population from which the sample was taken.

steps.title: Evaluate what each choice represents
steps.content:
• Choice A: All students from Ohio high schools — This is too broad. The sample came from only one school, not randomly from all Ohio schools.
  • Choice B: The 80 students themselves — This is the sample, not a generalization target.
  • Choice C: All students from the high school — This is the source population from which the 80 were randomly selected.
  • Choice D: The 32 students who identified the food — This is a subset of the sample, not a population for generalization.

steps.title: Determine the maximum scope of valid generalization
steps.content: The fundamental principle: a random sample generalizes to the population from which it was randomly drawn, but no further. The 80 students were randomly selected from one high school. Therefore, the results of the experiment can be generalized to all students in that high school. We cannot claim the findings hold for other Ohio high schools (different populations), and we must not mistake the sample for a population or a sample subset for the target of generalization. That matches choice C — C is correct.
